name: Tips for the ABM
recipeInstructions:
1. The best flour to use for bread machines is bread or high gluten flour. This flour contains the proper amount of protein and gluten to allow for lengthy kneading, stretching, and rising times without producing a small or collapsed loaf.
2. The loaf should be a good raiser with a fine grain texture.
3. Additional flours such as rye, oats, soy, cornmeal, whole wheat, rice, etc., may be added to any recipe without increasing the size of the breads. These flours are deficient in gluten (except for whole wheat which ironically has sufficient gluten but has a sharp gluten structure that punctures and deflates the risen yeast cells).
4. You can successfully add 1 or 2 cups of these flours to recipes which use 3 cups of bread flour.
5. The lightest and highest loaves are ones made with white bread flour, eggs, and liquids such as milk or water.
6. The smaller denser loaves are made with rye and whole wheat flour etc. These breads can even be made lighter if the flour is blended before using.
7. Blended oatmeal also makes a lighter textured bread.
8. The best bread machine cycle for denser loaves is the standard mode. The turbo or quick mode is excellent for basic white, French, Italian, or light weight breads. Become familiar with cycles on your own machine.
9. Humidity and temperature affect flour and dough consistency may never be the same in repeated recipes. Let your eyes be your guide to know when to add more flour or water to the dough in the kneading cycle. The dough should be a smooth, somewhat tacky dough when touched. However, no dough should cling to your fingers when touched.
10. Add flour or water gradually if needed in small amounts to get the proper consistency.
11. All ingredients should be lukewarm (not hot) when added to the recipe. People have the tendency to make the water much too hot for the bread machine, which activates the yeast too quickly and causes a poor textured loaf that may often collapse. Too hot water also may rupture the yeast cells and render them ineffective.
12. Temperatures over 110°F may be too hot.
13. There are many baker's yeasts on the market. Red Star is reputed to make breads rise higher than Fleischmans yeast. SAF instant yeast (French imported yeast) produces higher, better textured bread using less yeast than called for in recipes. Judge for yourself by experimenting.
14. Ingredients which help improve texture, rising, and character of bread:
- GLUTEN FLOUR or powder: very high in protein and helps breads rise higher. You don't need this if using a good brand of bread or high gluten flour. The protein adds a nutritional boost to breads. Available in health food stores.
- PURE BARLEY MALT SYRUP: A tablespoon or so also helps breads rise higher as the sugars feed the yeast. Found in health food stores.
- LIQUID LECITHIN: improves the textures of the breads, making them stay fresh longer. Found in health food stores.
- VITAMIN C (ascorbic acid): crush a tablet or use it in powder form to help breads have a better texture and mellow gluten.
- FRESH LEMON JUICE: a few teaspoons also mellows the gluten and helps the texture of the bread-making for a good keeper and raiser.
15. Other miscellaneous tips for the breadmaker:
- Use baking spray to spray the globe tops of bread machines in case your breads over rise and stick. This aids in cleanup!
- Use a small pastry or paintbrush to sweep away the bread crumbs inside your machine and use a damp paper towel to pick them up.
- Low-fat products such as skim milk, egg beaters, light margarine, low/non-fat sour cream, etc., work well but may result in a different texture for higher fat products. Bread may be denser and smaller.
- If bread does not come out satisfactory, it can be used for breadcrumbs, bread pudding, bread dumplings, or given to birds!
6. A general safe ratio for breadmaking is: 3 cups bread flour, 1 1/4 cups liquid, 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar, and 2 tsp yeast. Experiment from there. Decrease the total amount of liquids if added oil and eggs are used.
7. It pays to buy yeast and flour in large quantities if making a lot of bread. Store flour in a cool dry place. Yeast may be refrigerated.
8. Cool breads at least 30 minutes to 1 hour before slicing.
9. Any breads that don't seem quite baked, place them in a conventional oven set to 350°F for 10 minutes to finish baking.
10. Eggs: When directions call for 1/2 of an egg, etc., just use one small egg. Normally, large eggs are used in all baking.
11. Too much garlic and cinnamon decreases the power of yeast in a bread recipe. A small amount is okay when added directly with ingredients but don't go overboard if you're a garlic or cinnamon lover. Add the extras in the last kneading cycle.
